body.body:
From Akien 2026-04-14 morning, refined across four exchanges.

## Definition
**COA = Center of Attention = NE + TWM + anything that's part of 'what am I aware of'.** This is the proper noun for the unit that can exist in parallel. COAs bundle a narrative engine with its own TWM slice and any attentional state that defines its current awareness.

## Current state
- **akiendelllinux** currently runs **ONE COA** (the main Igor).
- When akiendell (or any additional machine) spins up, the distributed Igor has **TWO COAs** running concurrently.
- Cross-machine per-instance MCPs already exist (mcp__igor_akiendell__*, mcp__igor_yoga9i__*, mcp__igor_yogai7__*) and provide addressability.
- **Shared milieu at master DB level already exists** — one emotional milieu bubbles up to individual COAs gradually. Not a design question, existing substrate.
- What does NOT yet exist: the unified COA abstraction, the spawn primitive, and **inter-COA messaging (the one piece Akien flagged as genuinely new).**

## Three pieces this ticket covers

### 1. COA spawn primitive (load-gated, fluid allocation)
Like spawning a tool or a CC minion, but cognitive: Igor can open a new COA for a set of work. Parent COA keeps running. Child COA runs with its own cursor + TWM slice, sharing the graph matrix. Completion = memory deposit in parent COA's TWM at moderate salience — 'enough to draw attention, not enough to demand it.' No callback wiring — parent's next salience competition picks up the completion marker naturally.

**Not one COA per task.** Allocation is fluid and portfolio-shaped. A foreground Igor can spawn ONE background Igor to handle open tickets + reading + training as a mixed portfolio. Background work is slow because background — that's the nature of it, not a bug. The division is by **task category / mode**, not per-item.

**Concurrency is load-gated**, not capped at a fixed number. Hardware is the constraint; resource checks (CPU, memory, GPU, db load) gate new spawns. Current hardware comfortably runs ~2 concurrent COAs; Igor needs to understand this about himself rather than have it hardcoded.

### 2. Shared milieu — already in place
Individual COAs share ONE emotional milieu at the master DB level. Global milieu bubbles up into individual COAs gradually — not instantaneously, so a stressed reading COA doesn't flip the conversing COA into stress mode immediately. This already works. **No design question, no new implementation — verification and documentation only.**

### 3. Inter-COA conversation (the genuinely new piece)
COAs must be able to talk to each other. Canonical example: a **conversing COA** asks a **reading COA** 'how's the reading going?' and gets back a useful answer without interrupting the reading COA's focus.

**Constraints settled by biomimicry:**
- Async messaging (biology is async; matches 'draw attention not demand attention')
- Asked COA answers from its own current TWM state — the reading COA doesn't stop reading, it responds with what's currently in awareness
- Same protocol for same-box and cross-box (per-machine MCPs already give cross-box addressability; same-box just needs a local COA id)
- COA identity on cross-box reuses what memory_sync already uses for instance identity

**Open design question (genuinely unresolved, needs Akien):**
- Message mechanism: is an inter-COA message a deposit into the target COA's TWM with a reply flag? A new memory type? A ring_message category? Small choice but pick one.

## Biomimicry framing
Parallel centers of attention — like parallel cortical activations sharing the same substrate. Each has its own salience budget (own, not shared — shared pool would reintroduce the competition we're trying to avoid). Tier/model ceiling inherits from mode (AWAKE/BG/SLEEP), not from parent COA — mode is the authority on routing. Nesting is allowed: sub-foci within foci, arbitrated by the same salience competition. Coordination emerges from TWM salience competition + the shared milieu, not from scripted handoff.

## Far-future framing (not this ticket, but sets direction)
When the graph matrix is populated enough that Igor no longer needs the LLM to simulate Igor — when Igor lives in the graph — the LLM becomes an upstream consultant that Igor calls as a trusted advisor rather than the substrate running him. COA + inter-COA messaging is a step toward that world: the COAs are Igor, the LLM is what one or more of them occasionally consults.

## Why this matters
- Unlocks BACKGROUND mode (T-igor-modes #406) non-intrusion — a focused COA isn't interrupted by 'check the reading queue'; reading is in another COA's portfolio
- Formalizes multi-machine Igor: akiendelllinux + akiendell + yoga9i + yogai7 become a coherent N-COA system, not N isolated instances
- Enables dream/watchdog/reading COAs to run in parallel with the conversing COA during SLEEP/BACKGROUND
- Inter-COA messaging is the new primitive that lets COAs check on each other without breaking each other's focus

## Existing work to check
- **tools/deferred_self_task.py** — push_deferred_result_to_twm does result→parent-TWM on completion. Result-routing primitive exists.
- **worker_foreman.py** — queue + PID tracking + liveness. Spawn substrate.
- **NE TraversalCursor (#236)** — parent ticket for the NE abstraction.
- **T-task-chain (#285)**, **T-worker-queue (#286)**, **T-igor-poll-spawn (#287)** — prior worker-spawn tickets; likely subsumed once the COA abstraction is clear.
- **memory_sync (#293, D169)** — swarm sync substrate for cross-box.
- **cluster_ssh + swarm** — existing multi-machine infra.
- **milieu object** — verify scope and bubble-up mechanics; document how it becomes 'master DB level' shared across COAs (Akien says it's already there).
- **Per-machine MCPs** — already provide cross-machine addressability. Probably the inter-COA message-passing primitive already, just needs COA-level wrapping.

## Remaining design questions needing Akien
1. **Inter-COA message mechanism** — TWM deposit + reply flag, new memory type, ring_message category, or other? (Decided: async. Undecided: concrete shape.)
2. **Load gating thresholds** — what CPU/memory/db-load levels should block a new spawn? Or start with 'try, fall back if it fails'?

## Dependencies
- **T-igor-modes (#406)** co-evolves with this. Prerequisite for BACKGROUND mode non-intrusion.
- Parent-absorbs T-task-chain (#285), T-worker-queue (#286), T-igor-poll-spawn (#287) once the COA abstraction stabilizes.
body.notes:
AUTHORED from Akien dictation 2026-04-19 (recovering content from 2026-04-18 conversation that never flushed).

## The architecture (already partially exists)

Multiple Igor instances already run concurrently — one per box across 4 boxes (akiendelllinux, akiendell, yoga9i, yogai7). Their emotional milieus influence one another biologically: mostly but not completely cut off. That cross-instance milieu behavior is the template.

## What changes in this ticket

**Allow more than one Igor instance ON THE SAME BOX.** Typical case:
  - one Igor in front (chatting with the human)
  - one running background (reading, research)
  - others for specialized tasks
Not dozens per box — CPU load gates spawn decisions.

## What must move

If the master TWM loop lives in main.py, it has to move out — multiple instances in one process means multiple loops running at once. TWM becomes per-instance; shared state (cortex, milieu propagation) needs to support multiple readers/writers.

## Answers to the four open questions

1. **Trigger** — Igor decides to spawn a new COA. Not user-initiated. A boredom engram or an inner-state signal ("I want to read while chatting") drives it.
2. **Milieu sharing** — same pattern as cross-box: each COA has its own milieu; they influence one another biologically (partial propagation, not a shared state). That code already exists for cross-box — reuse/extend for intra-box.
3. **Inter-COA messaging** — Igor-mediated "nudge" pushes. When something highly relevant in context 1 surfaces in context 2, the instance pushes it across. Not a new comms-channel primitive — a TWM-level nudge, biologically salience-driven.
4. **Termination** — self-terminate when focus is satisfied; CPU-load watchdog can cull if the box gets thrashed.

## Related
- T-retire-worker-foreman — retires the foreman/daemon that currently serializes workers; COAs naturally replace that pattern.
- T-swarm-manager — fleet-wide visibility of all COAs across all boxes.

## Provenance
Akien dictation, 2026-04-19 autonomous sprint. Prior content was lost in 2026-04-18 context compaction before flushing.
body.title: CLOSED: Concurrent Centers-of-Attention (COA) — NE + TWM spawning
body.result: Two child tickets shipped: T-coa-object-wrap (7557a93b) + T-coa-spawn-primitive (1dc0255a). COA abstraction live; spawn primitive + CPU gate + dissolve in place. Inter-COA messaging and boredom-triggered spawn are future tickets.
body.description:
AUTHORED from Akien dictation 2026-04-19 (recovering content from 2026-04-18 conversation that never flushed).

## The architecture (already partially exists)

Multiple Igor instances already run concurrently — one per box across 4 boxes (akiendelllinux, akiendell, yoga9i, yogai7). Their emotional milieus influence one another biologically: mostly but not completely cut off. That cross-instance milieu behavior is the template.

## What changes in this ticket

**Allow more than one Igor instance ON THE SAME BOX.** Typical case:
  - one Igor in front (chatting with the human)
  - one running background (reading, research)
  - others for specialized tasks
Not dozens per box — CPU load gates spawn decisions.

## What must move

If the master TWM loop lives in main.py, it has to move out — multiple instances in one process means multiple loops running at once. TWM becomes per-instance; shared state (cortex, milieu propagation) needs to support multiple readers/writers.

## Answers to the four open questions

1. **Trigger** — Igor decides to spawn a new COA. Not user-initiated. A boredom engram or an inner-state signal ("I want to read while chatting") drives it.
2. **Milieu sharing** — same pattern as cross-box: each COA has its own milieu; they influence one another biologically (partial propagation, not a shared state). That code already exists for cross-box — reuse/extend for intra-box.
3. **Inter-COA messaging** — Igor-mediated "nudge" pushes. When something highly relevant in context 1 surfaces in context 2, the instance pushes it across. Not a new comms-channel primitive — a TWM-level nudge, biologically salience-driven.
4. **Termination** — self-terminate when focus is satisfied; CPU-load watchdog can cull if the box gets thrashed.

## Related
- T-retire-worker-foreman — retires the foreman/daemon that currently serializes workers; COAs naturally replace that pattern.
- T-swarm-manager — fleet-wide visibility of all COAs across all boxes.

## Provenance
Akien dictation, 2026-04-19 autonomous sprint. Prior content was lost in 2026-04-18 context compaction before flushing.

**Current state (already shipped):** concurrent Igor instances across boxes sharing the common home DB — one COA per laptop. Cross-box milieu propagation works. That cross-box mechanism is the template this ticket reuses for intra-box.

**What this ticket changes:**
- Move COA/TWM out of main.py into a `COA` object so multiple instances can run in one process
- Each box still gets a root COA by default
- Can spin up additional COAs for background tasks (e.g. one chatting with a human in the foreground, one reading in the background)
- Max ~4 COAs per box (CPU gate enforces, but practical ceiling is small)
- Background COAs that run out of things to do just sleep and dissolve — they do **not** get bored. Boredom is a foreground-COA primitive; background COAs are task-scoped and exit cleanly when their task is exhausted.

**Affected files:** wild_igor/igor/main.py (extract the TWM loop into a COA object — this is the central refactor); wild_igor/igor/cognition/coa.py (new — COA object holding NE+TWM+milieu reference); wild_igor/igor/cognition/narrative_engine.py + wild_igor/igor/cognition/twm.py (per-COA instances, no global state); wild_igor/igor/cognition/milieu.py (extend cross-box propagation primitive to intra-box COAs); a spawn primitive in tools/ops.py or tools/spawn_coa.py.

**Design rules:** docs-in-code (coa.py + main.py refactor are HIGH-inertia — refresh top-of-file docstrings); test-plan-or-why-not; no-sqlite.

**Scope boundary:** IN — per-COA NE+TWM instances, intra-box milieu propagation reuse, Igor-decided spawn trigger, CPU-load gate, sleep-and-dissolve on background COA task exhaustion. OUT — dozens-per-box runtime tuning (4 is the practical ceiling); cross-COA arbitration policies beyond milieu propagation; GUI surfacing of multiple COAs.

**Test plan:** tests/test_coa_spawn.py — spawn a 2nd COA on same box, assert NE/TWM isolation, assert intra-box milieu propagation between them; CPU-load gate refuses 5th COA; background COA sleeps+dissolves after task queue empty. Existing single-COA tests still pass.

## Design questions — RESOLVED 2026-05-07

**Inter-COA message mechanism:** Shared cortex model — no explicit inter-COA messaging primitive. COAs share the same memory graph. A HUGE event in COA 1 deposits into cortex at high salience; all attached COAs surface it naturally on next salience competition. Same mechanism as cross-box milieu propagation (already implemented). COA 1 does not ask COA 2 anything — COA 2 just reads COA 1's memories as its own. No new memory type, no ring_message category, no TWM deposit-with-reply-flag needed.

**Load gate thresholds:** CPU > 60% over the last 60 seconds blocks new COA spawn. Adjustable via env var IGOR_COA_CPU_GATE (default 60, percent). Ollama + Igor together consume ~50% when busy — this gate keeps the box from thrashing while allowing spawns during quiet periods.

**Rollback plan:** git revert on the COA extraction commit restores main.py to single-COA mode. The COA object wraps the existing NE+TWM loop with no behavior change for the single-COA case — revert is safe.

**HIGH-inertia pre-approval (Akien 2026-05-07):** Approved to touch main.py (extract TWM loop into COA object), narrative_engine.py, twm.py, milieu.py. Scope: single-COA behavior unchanged; new spawn primitive added on top.
